food_subgroup: Lamb / mutton dishes — spiced and broiled

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb chop food product class; no specific masala-spiced variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific masala lamb chop entry. The sheep/lamb meat parent category A00LC is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for masala lamb chops as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT is not used here.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of lamb chops masala consists of approximately 2 to 3 bone-in lamb chops with tomatoes, onion, and herbs, weighing approximately 300 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton chops, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports cooked mutton/lamb chop values per 100 g edible portion. Values adapted for masala-spiced broiled preparation; spice and vegetable additions (tomato, onion, herbs) contribute minor adjustments to carbohydrate and sodium. Core macros anchored to ICMR-NIN cooked lamb/mutton chop data. Sodium elevated slightly to reflect masala spice blend and salt in marinade.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Meat and Protein-Rich Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb and other red meats are protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible glycemic carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) consistently assign GI values of approximately 0–15 to cooked meat dishes. A GI of 15 is used as a conservative low estimate for this spiced lamb dish, reflecting the minor carbohydrate contribution from masala spices, tomato, and onion. GL per serving = (15 × 3.2 × 300/100) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from masala spice blend and marinade salt
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, lamb chops masala has a negligible glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol content from lamb
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb chops contain approximately 5.8 g saturated fat and 82 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one to two chops and prefer lean cuts with visible fat trimmed to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Chops Masala (Spicy) is a North Indian restaurant dish consisting of bone-in lamb chops that are marinated in a spiced yogurt-based masala blend — typically incorporating ginger, garlic, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and turmeric — and then broiled or grilled at high heat to achieve a charred, aromatic exterior while retaining a juicy, tender interior. The dish is served with fresh tomatoes, sliced onion, and herbs such as coriander leaf. The flavour profile is bold, smoky, and intensely spiced, with a pronounced heat from chilli and a deep savoury richness from the lamb fat and masala marinade. Nutritionally, lamb chops masala is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible, which is favourable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. However, the sodium content is elevated due to the spice marinade and salt, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The saturated fat content from lamb warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease. This dish is a staple of North Indian cuisine and is commonly found in Indian restaurants across Singapore, particularly in establishments serving Mughlai and Punjabi-style cooking. It is halal-certified in most Singapore Indian Muslim restaurants. The dish is rich in iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus, reflecting the nutritional profile of red meat.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton/lamb chop data (2017 revised edition), which is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. The specific restaurant preparation (Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, Singapore) is not directly measured; values reflect a defensible adaptation of the ICMR-NIN cooked lamb chop profile with minor adjustments for masala spicing, tomato, onion, and salt in the marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high because no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish exists in an approved primary source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods source for cooked lamb/mutton chops. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with best-available estimates from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy lamb data. Sodium is elevated above the ICMR-NIN baseline to reflect the masala marinade and added salt typical of restaurant preparation. The food name contains a typographic character 'ç' in the original input description ('çhops'); this has been corrected to 'chops' in all output fields. This dish is served at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore (field survey data source). Price SGD 35.90. The GI of 15 is a conservative analogous estimate for a protein- and fat-dominant meat dish with minor carbohydrate from spices and vegetables; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). GL per 300 g serving = (15 × 3.2 × 3) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4.
